exercises.title: ft_putchar
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays a character on the standard output.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_putchar(char c);
```
**Constraints:**
You can only use `write` from `<unistd.h>`.

exercises.title: ft_print_alphabet
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays the alphabet in lowercase, on a single line.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_alphabet(void);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_reverse_alphabet
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays the alphabet in reverse order (from 'z' to 'a').

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_reverse_alphabet(void);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_numbers
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays all digits from 0 to 9.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_numbers(void);
```

exercises.title: ft_is_negative
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays 'N' if the integer is negative and 'P' if positive or null.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_is_negative(int n);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_comb
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays all different combinations of three digits (ascending).

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_comb(void);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_comb2
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays all different combinations of two two-digit numbers (00 01 to 98 99).

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_comb2(void);
```

exercises.title: ft_putnbr
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays an integer on the standard output.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_putnbr(int nb);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_combn
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays all combinations of `n` different digits in ascending order.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_print_combn(int n);
```

exercises.title: ft_ft
exercises.description:
Write a function that takes a pointer to int as a parameter and sets the value pointed to to 42.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_ft(int *nbr);
```

exercises.title: ft_ultimate_ft
exercises.description:
Write a function that takes a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to a pointer to an int, and sets the value pointed to to 42.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_ultimate_ft(int *********nbr);
```

exercises.title: ft_swap
exercises.description:
Write a function that swaps the value of two integers.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_swap(int *a, int *b);
```

exercises.title: ft_div_mod
exercises.description:
Write a function that divides parameters and stores the result in the int pointed to by `div`, and the remainder in the int pointed to by `mod`.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_div_mod(int a, int b, int *div, int *mod);
```

exercises.title: ft_ultimate_div_mod
exercises.description:
Write a function that divides the value in `a` by the value in `b`. Store the result in `a` and the remainder in `b`.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_ultimate_div_mod(int *a, int *b);
```

exercises.title: ft_putstr
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays a string of characters.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_putstr(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_strlen
exercises.description:
Write a function that counts and returns the number of characters in a string.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_strlen(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_rev_int_tab
exercises.description:
Write a function that reverses the order of elements in an array of integers.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_rev_int_tab(int *tab, int size);
```

exercises.title: ft_sort_int_tab
exercises.description:
Write a function that sorts an array of integers in ascending order.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_sort_int_tab(int *tab, int size);
```

exercises.title: ft_strcpy
exercises.description:
Reproduce the behavior of the function `strcpy`.

**Function prototype:**
```c

```

exercises.title: ft_strncpy
exercises.description:
Reproduce the behavior of the function `strncpy`.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strncpy(char *dest, char *src, unsigned int n);
```

exercises.title: ft_str_is_alpha
exercises.description:
Returns 1 if the string contains only alphabetical characters, 0 otherwise.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_str_is_alpha(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_str_is_numeric
exercises.description:
Returns 1 if the string contains only digits, 0 otherwise.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_str_is_numeric(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_str_is_lowercase
exercises.description:
Returns 1 if the string contains only lowercase letters, 0 otherwise.

exercises.description:
Returns 1 if the string contains only printable characters, 0 otherwise.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_str_is_printable(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_strupcase
exercises.description:
Converts all lowercase letters of a string to uppercase.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strupcase(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_strlowcase
exercises.description:
Converts all uppercase letters of a string to lowercase.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strlowcase(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_strcapitalize
exercises.description:
Capitalizes the first letter of each word in a string.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strcapitalize(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_strlcpy
exercises.description:
Reproduce the behavior of the function `strlcpy`.

```c
unsigned int ft_strlcpy(char *dest, char *src, unsigned int size);
```

exercises.title: ft_putstr_non_printable
exercises.description:
Displays a string, replacing non-printable characters with backslash followed by two hex digits.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_putstr_non_printable(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_print_memory
exercises.description:
Displays the memory content of a given address in hexadecimal and ASCII format.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void *ft_print_memory(void *addr, unsigned int size);
```

```c
char *ft_strstr(char *str, char *to_find);
```

```c
unsigned int ft_strlcat(char *dest, char *src, unsigned int size);
```

Reproduce the behavior of the function `atoi`.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_atoi(char *str);
```

exercises.title: ft_putnbr_base
exercises.description:
Display a number in a given base.

**Function prototype:**
```c
void ft_putnbr_base(int nbr, char *base);
```

exercises.title: ft_atoi_base
exercises.description:
Reproduce the behavior of `atoi`, but with a given base.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_atoi_base(char *str, char *base);
```

exercises.title: ft_iterative_factorial
exercises.description:
Returns the factorial of a number using an iterative method.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_iterative_factorial(int nb);
```

exercises.title: ft_recursive_factorial
exercises.description:
Returns the factorial of a number using recursion.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_recursive_factorial(int nb);
```

exercises.title: ft_iterative_power
exercises.description:
Returns the result of a number raised to a power using an iterative method.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_iterative_power(int nb, int power);
```

exercises.title: ft_recursive_power
exercises.description:
Returns the result of a number raised to a power using recursion.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_recursive_power(int nb, int power);
```

exercises.title: ft_fibonacci
exercises.description:
Returns the nth Fibonacci number.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_fibonacci(int index);
```

exercises.title: ft_sqrt
exercises.description:
Returns the square root of a number if it is a perfect square, otherwise returns 0.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_sqrt(int nb);
```

exercises.title: ft_ten_queens_puzzle
exercises.description:
Write a function that displays all the possible positions of 10 queens on a 10×10 chessboard so that no two queens threaten each other. A solution is displayed as a sequence of 10 digits followed by a newline, where each digit represents the row position of a queen in each column (from column 0 to column 9).

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_ten_queens_puzzle(void);
```

The function should return the total number of solutions found.

exercises.title: ft_print_program_name
exercises.description:
Write a program that displays its own name followed by a newline.

**Constraints:**
- Only use `write` from `<unistd.h>`.
- `argv[0]` contains the program name.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int main(int argc, char **argv);
```
exercises.tests:
Compile with:
```bash
gcc -Wall -Wextra -Werror ft_print_program_name.c -o print_name
```
Run:
```bash
./print_name
```

exercises.title: ft_print_param
exercises.description:
Write a program that displays its arguments, one per line, in the order they were given.

**Constraints:**
- Use `write` from `<unistd.h>` only.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int main(int argc, char **argv);
```
exercises.tests:
Compile with:
```bash
gcc -Wall -Wextra -Werror ft_print_param.c -o print_param
```
Run with parameters:
```bash
./print_param Hello world "42 School"
```
Expected Output:
```
Hello
world
42 School
```

exercises.title: ft_rev_params
exercises.description:
Write a program that displays its arguments in reverse order, one per line.

**Constraints:**
- Use `write` from `<unistd.h>` only.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int main(int argc, char **argv);
```
exercises.tests:
Compile with:
```bash
gcc -Wall -Wextra -Werror ft_rev_params.c -o rev_params
```
Run with parameters:
```bash
./rev_params Hello world "42 School"
```
Expected Output:
```
42 School
world
Hello
```

exercises.title: ft_sort_params
exercises.description:
Write a program that sorts its arguments in ASCII order and displays them, one per line.

**Constraints:**
- Use only `write` from `<unistd.h>`.
- You must implement your own string comparison and swap functions.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int main(int argc, char **argv);
```
exercises.tests:
Compile with:
```bash
gcc -Wall -Wextra -Werror ft_sort_params.c -o sort_params
```
Run with:
```bash
./sort_params zebra apple Orange
```
Expected Output:
```
Orange
apple
zebra
```

exercises.title: ft_strdup
exercises.description:
Reproduce the behavior of the function `strdup`.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strdup(char *src);
```
**Constraints:**
- You must allocate memory using `malloc`.
- Do not use the standard `strdup` function.

exercises.title: ft_range
exercises.description:
Create an array of ints containing all values from `min` (inclusive) to `max` (exclusive).

**Function prototype:**
```c
int *ft_range(int min, int max);
```
**Constraints:**
- Return `NULL` if `min >= max`.
- You must allocate memory using `malloc`.

exercises.title: ft_ultimate_range
exercises.description:
Allocate and assign a range of integers to a pointer-to-pointer and return the size.

**Function prototype:**
```c
int ft_ultimate_range(int **range, int min, int max);
```
**Constraints:**
- Return `0` and set `*range` to `NULL` if `min >= max`.
- Return `-1` on allocation failure.

exercises.title: ft_strjoin
exercises.description:
Concatenate all strings in `strs` array with `sep` between them.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_strjoin(int size, char **strs, char *sep);
```
**Constraints:**
- If `size` is `0`, return an empty string (not `NULL`).

exercises.title: ft_convert_base
exercises.description:
Convert a number string from a base to another base.

**Function prototype:**
```c
char *ft_convert_base(char *nbr, char *base_from, char *base_to);
```
**Constraints:**
- You must handle negative numbers.
- Validate base (minimum 2 chars, no `+`, `-`, or duplicates).

exercises.title: ft_point
ft_strs_to_tab
ft_split
exercises.description:
Split a string into an array of words separated by a given charset.

**Function:**
```c
char **ft_split(char *str, char *charset);
```
